did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a hybrid deep learning model combining VGG-16 and EfficientNet architectures for skin cancer detection. The study used this AI model to classify dermoscopic images into benign and malignant lesions, achieving an accuracy of 96.3%, recall of 94.4%, and precision of 95.7%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is published in the Proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS-2025). It presents original experimental work, proposing a hybrid deep learning model (VGG-16 and EfficientNet) for skin cancer detection. The study includes a methodology section describing data collection, pre-processing, model training, and evaluation, along with specific quantitative results (accuracy 96.3%, recall 94.4%, precision 95.7%) and a confusion matrix, indicating it produces new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Revolutionizing Skin Cancer Detection: Leveraging Machine Learning and Deep Learning for Precise Lesion Identification
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning model combining VGG-16 and EfficientNet architectures to classify dermoscopic skin images into benign and malignant lesions. The research aims to enhance diagnostic accessibility and accuracy by leveraging the feature extraction capabilities of VGG-16 and the optimized scaling of EfficientNet. The model was evaluated using cross-validation and compared against baseline methods including SVM, U-Net, CNN, and K-Nearest Neighbor. The proposed hybrid model achieved an accuracy of 96.3%, with a recall of 94.4% and precision of 95.7%. These results indicate that the system effectively distinguishes between lesion types, potentially reducing reliance on invasive procedures and specialist availability.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a malignant lesion type in the classification task. The paper notes it is one of the most prevalent and deadly malignancies and focuses on distinguishing it from benign lesions.
Listed as one of the forms of skin cancer the research focuses on identifying and classifying, alongside squamous cell carcinoma and melanoma.
Listed as one of the forms of skin cancer the research focuses on identifying and classifying, alongside basal cell carcinoma and melanoma.
Used as the comparator class to malignant lesions in the binary classification task. The dataset (HAM10000) contains images divided into benign and malignant sections.
Used as the target class to benign lesions in the binary classification task. The model aims to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions.

Mentioned in the introduction regarding global incidence statistics (2-3 million cases annually) but not explicitly listed as a specific class label in the experimental classification task, which focuses on melanoma, BCC, and SCC.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a hybrid deep learning model (VGG-16 and EfficientNet) to classify dermoscopic skin images into discrete diagnostic categories: benign and malignant lesions. The study reports classification metrics including accuracy (96.3%), precision (95.7%), and recall (94.4%). While the introduction mentions other skin cancer types (BCC, SCC, Melanoma), the experimental evaluation focuses on the binary distinction between benign and malignant classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, described as a public dataset containing clinically reviewed dermoscopy images divided into benign and malignant sections. The paper notes it contains thousands of images with varying sizes (600x450 to 1024x768 pixels) and unequal prevalence of skin malignancies.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid deep learning model (VGG-16 and EfficientNet). Data augmentation (rotation, zooming, flipping) was applied to increase diversification and avoid over-fitting.
use.validation: Used for validation via cross-validation to minimize over-fitting.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the model's performance (accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, AUC-ROC).

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: Referenced as a public dataset containing clinically reviewed dermoscopy images with thousands of images divided into benign and malignant sections, used to increase dataset diversification.

use.training: Mentioned alongside HAM10000 as a source for increasing dataset diversification; specific usage details (e.g., if it was merged with HAM10000 or used separately) are not explicitly detailed beyond this general statement.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to minimize over-fitting during model training.
Used to evaluate actual vs predicted classifications and identify the model's ability to distinguish between classes.
Used to evaluate the performance of the model compared with baseline methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compared against baseline models via a confusion matrix and AUC-ROC, but no statistical significance testing was employed.

HAM10000.notes: The hybrid model demonstrated improved accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity compared to baseline models (SVM, CNN, KNN, U-Net). The study utilized cross-validation to minimize over-fitting. The dataset contained images of varying sizes (600x450 to 1024x768 pixels) with unequal prevalence of skin malignancies.

ISIC.metric: Not explicitly reported as separate quantitative results
ISIC.notes: ISIC is mentioned as a public dataset used alongside HAM10000 to increase dataset diversification. Specific quantitative results for ISIC alone are not provided in the text; results are aggregated or attributed to the combined/preprocessed dataset evaluation.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Model combining VGG-16 and EfficientNet

architecture_details.description: A hybrid design integrating VGG-16’s feature extraction capabilities with EfficientNet’s optimized scaling for depth, width, and resolution. VGG-16 is used for detecting complex, high-level characteristics, while EfficientNet balances computational efficiency and accuracy.

backbone_details.VGG-16: Used for powerful feature extraction and defining features of minimal size in medical images.
backbone_details.EfficientNet: Used for optimized scaling of depth, width, and resolution to make the algorithm computationally friendly and accurate.

classification_or_output_head.details: Classifies dermoscopic skin images into benign and malignant lesions.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including rotation, zooming, and flipping to mimic variations of skin conditions and avoid over-fitting
Feature augmentation with patient age, gender, lesion location, and area of interest lesion type

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model combining VGG-16 and EfficientNet architectures achieved high diagnostic performance for skin cancer detection, with an accuracy of 96.3%, precision of 95.7%, and recall of 94.4%.

The hybrid model demonstrated improved accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity compared to baseline models including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and U-Net.

The study concludes that AI-powered skin cancer detection systems can reduce the expenses involved in pathological testing, increase accessibility to diagnosis, and improve early and efficient disease diagnosis.

The research highlights that current diagnostic techniques are limited by accessibility and human error, whereas the proposed AI system offers a reliable alternative for timely detection of malignant and benign lesions.

Future research directions identified by the authors include extending the capability of the model to detect a range of conditions beyond skin cancer and finding ways to interpret the model’s results better to enhance interpretability.
important_remarks: The study relies on publicly available datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC) which may introduce population bias and does not report external validation on independent clinical cohorts.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance differences against baseline models.

The proposed hybrid model (VGG-16 + EfficientNet) is noted to have increased computational expenses and a larger model size, posing potential challenges for real-time or resource-constrained deployment.
Data augmentation techniques were limited to rotation, zooming, and flipping; no advanced augmentation or domain adaptation strategies were employed to address potential domain shifts.
The binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) simplifies the clinical reality where specific subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. BCC vs. SCC) have different prognoses and management strategies.

The paper mentions feature augmentation with patient age, gender, and lesion location but does not provide details on how these non-image features were integrated into the deep learning architecture or their impact on performance.
No analysis of model interpretability or explainability (XAI) was performed, despite the paper acknowledging it as a future direction, which limits clinical trust and adoption.
